title: The rule of law in th'WPS is ours—no ifs and buts'

Duterte's 'delicate balancing act' and Ecclesiastes 3 invocation
Carpio's instant rebuttal on constitutional limits to allowing foreign nationals to fish in the EEZ
CJ's defense: liberal interpretation + Senate two-thirds concurrence safeguard

Traditional fishing rights allowed to foreigners only in archipelagic waters and territorial sea

signature_phrases.phrase: During his State of the Nation Address (Sona) on July 22, President Duterte made it very clear
the West Philippine Sea is ours. There [are] no ifs and buts. It is ours
we have been acting [applause] along that legal truth and line. But we have to temper it with the times and the realities that we face today
in my estimate, about 18 percent of its length or roughly 17 of his 93 minutes of delivery time
the avoidance of conflict â€” armed conflict â€” and protection of our territorial waters and natural resources compel us to perform a delicate balancing act
more and better results can be reached in the privacy of a conference room than in a squabble in public
our ownership of the [WPS] is internationally recognized
fact-checking the President, Senior Justice Antonio T. Carpio instantly reacted
factually and legally, China is not in possession of the entire WPS
the learned justice may be correct in his textual reading of the Constitution
I think a liberal interpretation may allow the President, with the concurrence of 'at least two-thirds of all the Members of the Senate'
in my humble view, we need not fear that our President may have entered intoâ€¦ a lopsided agreement
such agreement or treaty, by the express wording of our Constitution, shall not be 'valid and effective' absent the Senate's concurrence
I am glad the President has boldly clarified his stance
paraphrasing Ecclesiastes 3 of the Good Book
everyone, including his critics, should be mollified that, to repeat, he has not waived our maritime rights or the arbitral award
I would rather focus on and offer a toast to his bold presidential incantation

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China â€” UNCLOS Annex VII arbitral award (July 12, 2016)

stances.claim: Duterte's SONA was unambiguous on WPS sovereignty â€” 'the West Philippine Sea is ours. There are no ifs and buts' â€” and the President devoted roughly 18% (â‰ˆ17 of 93 minutes) of his speech to this controversy, explaining that conflict-avoidance compels a delicate balancing act and that better results come from private conference rooms than from public squabbles.
stances.rhetorical_move: concession-counting of SONA content (specific quote + estimated air-time)

stances.claim: Carpio is correct in his textual reading that the Constitution reserves the use and enjoyment of EEZ marine wealth exclusively to Filipino citizens, and that traditional fishing rights for foreigners are allowed only in archipelagic waters and the territorial sea â€” but a liberal interpretation may permit the President, with the concurrence of at least two-thirds of the Senate, to enter into a treaty allowing other states to use such wealth.
stances.rhetorical_move: agree-then-qualify (concede the textual reading; offer the liberal-interpretive escape hatch)
stances.confidence: asserted with 'I think' and 'in my humble view' humility markers

stances.claim: Filipinos need not fear a lopsided Duterte-Xi private agreement because, by the express wording of the Constitution, any such treaty or agreement shall not be 'valid and effective' without Senate two-thirds concurrence.
By the SONA itself, Duterte has asserted â€” not waived â€” PH maritime rights and the arbitral award, leaving room for successors to enforce them aggressively via options short of war.
stances.rhetorical_move: preservation-of-rights argument (today's restraint does not foreclose tomorrow's enforcement)

stances.claim: Whatever critics say, presidential foreign-policy determination is the President's prerogative subject only to constitutional and statutory limits; criticisms 'amount to nothing unless he adopts or accepts them.'

notable_anecdotes.summary: CJ's own time-estimate of WPS coverage in the SONA â€” 'about 18 percent of its length or roughly 17 of his 93 minutes of delivery time' â€” the kind of measurable specificity that distinguishes CJ's commentary from mere reaction, with the precision itself functioning as the argument that the President took the issue seriously.

The Carpio rebuttal sequence â€” Carpio 'instantly reacted' to Duterte's SONA, CJ then 'fact-checking' Carpio while agreeing on the textual reading but offering the Senate-concurrence escape hatch â€” the most explicit doctrinal exchange between two of the most respected Filipino legal voices in the entire arc.

Duterte's Ecclesiastes 3 paraphrase â€” 'There is a time for everything. A time to negotiate and a time to quarrelâ€¦ a time to antagonize and a time to make peace and a time to go to war, and a time to live and a time to die' â€” quoted by CJ as the President's framing of why aggressive enforcement is deferred to 'in due time.'
The 'Cheers!' closer â€” distinctive of CJ's signature register, deployed here as a toast to Duterte's bold sovereignty assertion despite the column's earlier disagreement with parts of the SONA.

decision_framework_signals: applies content-analysis quantification of speeches (% of length and minutes) as evidence of presidential prioritization

treats today's diplomatic restraint as preservation, not waiver, of future enforcement options
applies the constitutional-allocation-of-power principle (presidential prerogative on foreign policy subject to constitutional/statutory limits)
applies 'liberal interpretation' as the default mode for resolving textual ambiguity in favor of executive flexibility
frames the President as 'asserting' rather than 'waiving' rights â€” even in conciliatory diplomacy

one_paragraph_summary: Reacting to Duterte's July 22, 2019 State of the Nation Address, Panganiban quotes the President's unambiguous declaration â€” 'the West Philippine Sea is ours. There are no ifs and buts. It is ours' â€” and estimates the speech devoted roughly 18% of its length (â‰ˆ17 of 93 minutes) to the WPS controversy. Duterte explained that conflict-avoidance and resource protection compel a 'delicate balancing act,' and that better results come from 'the privacy of a conference room than in a squabble in public,' paraphrasing Ecclesiastes 3 to defer aggressive enforcement to 'in due time.' Senior Justice Antonio T. Carpio reacted immediately, arguing that, factually, China does not possess the entire WPS, and that the President cannot by himself allow foreign nationals to fish in the EEZ because the Constitution (Art. XIII, Sec. 2, 2nd par.) reserves marine wealth exclusively to Filipinos. CJ fact-checks the fact-checker: Carpio is correct on the textual reading, but a liberal interpretation may permit the President â€” with two-thirds Senate concurrence â€” to enter into such a treaty. And because the Constitution makes any such agreement not 'valid and effective' without Senate concurrence, Filipinos need not fear a lopsided Xi-Duterte deal. By the SONA itself, CJ argues, Duterte has asserted â€” not waived â€” PH maritime rights and the arbitral award, preserving future enforcement options for his successors. He closes with a toast to the President's bold sovereignty incantation â€” 'Cheers!'
